item.title: 贝尔法斯特抗议者刺伤后骚乱再次爆发，警方向贝尔法斯特抗议者发射高压水枪
item.description:
BELFAST, Northern Ireland (AP) — Police blasted water cannons Wednesday at protesters in Northern Ireland who set small fires and hurled bricks, rocks and bottles at them during a second night of violence over a brutal stabbing on a Belfast street.
Demonstrators wearing masks tore bricks from the walls outside homes and smashed sidewalks with sledgehammers to toss at riot police. In one place, the unruly crowd used sections of a dismantled picket fence to take cover on the street.
The clashes with police came several hours after a 30-year-old man from Sudan appeared in a Belfast court charged with attempted murder in astabbing attackthat left a man seriously injured and triggered anti-immigrant violence.
Hadi Alodid, 30, was ordered held in jail after appearing by video in Belfast Magistrates’ Court, where a detective said he blinded Stephen Ogilvie in the left eye during the knife attack. He was also charged with possessing a knife and threatening to kill a radiographer while being treated for a hand injury after the assault.
When police arrived at the crime scene, they found Alodid on the man, armed with a kitchen knife, the detective said. Alodid later told hospital staff: “I’ve killed someone, I don’t know if they are dead,” and said, “I will kill you.”
He refused legal representation through an Arabic interpreter and did not enter a plea.
Police were prepared for more violence after masked men on Tuesday set fire to several homes they believed to house immigrants, burned trash bins, torched a Belfast bus and pelted police with objects.
Firefighters rescued several people from burning houses and more than two dozen people were left homeless.
Anselme Shima, a Belfast resident originally from Congo, said he saw smoke from burning vehicles near his home.
“I’ve lived on my street for almost 10 years, I have a good relationship with my neighbors, but last night was a horrific one,” he said. “We don’t know what to do. I’m scared. Seeing this, I’m wondering if I’m next.”
Families, one with a baby, were rescued and taken to police stations for safety, Police Service of Northern Ireland Chief Constable Jon Boutcher said.
“These weren’t just families from ethnic minority communities, these were families from across communities that were caught up in this vile behavior last night,” Boutcher told the BBC. “There is absolutely no excuse for it.”
Boutcher said 200 more officers would be on the streets Wednesday and the PSNI was calling in support from other forces. Bus and train operators in Belfast said they would stop services early because of expected protests.
Ogilvie’s family appealed for an end to the violence and said migrants “make a deeply valuable contribution to our country.”
“We do not want this terrible tragedy to be used to divide people or fuel hostility,” the family said in a statement.
Politicians from both parts of Northern Ireland’spower-sharing governmentcondemned the violence. First Minister Michelle O’Neill of Irish nationalist party Sinn Fein said it was “thuggery.”
“Groups of masked men burning families out of their homes is nothing less than disgusting cowardice,” she said.
Deputy First Minister Emma Little-Pengelly, of the pro-British Democratic Unionist Party, said that “taking frustration at the evil actions of a person out on those who had no part in it is utterly wrong.”
Monday’s attack, caught in video footage that quickly spread on social media, was seized on by anti-immigration activists. Ogilvie, a man in his 40s, was hospitalized with deep cuts to his head, face and back.
Police said Alodid entered Northern Ireland from the neighboring Republic of Ireland in 2023, applied for asylum and was given a five-year permit to remain.
The Police Service of Northern Ireland said there is no information to suggest the attack was terrorism-related.
Protests were encouraged online by far-right activists, and the street violence erupted despite politicians’ calls for calm.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer condemned the stabbing attack as “sickening,” but said violence against people based on their background would not be tolerated.
“The scenes in Belfast last night were shocking and completely unacceptable,” Starmer said on X. “There is no justification for the violence and disorder that we saw threatening our communities, nor for those who encouraged it, online or elsewhere.”
Northern Ireland Justice Minister Naomi Long said social media agitators who “yesterday would have struggled to find Belfast on a map” were “weaponizing” the fears of local people.
“If you’re driving people from their homes based on nothing but the color of their skin, you can’t dress that up any other way, it’s racism, and those bad faith actors need to take a step back,” she told the BBC.
Some politicians said the stabbing should spark a review of the open border between Northern Ireland, which is part of the U.K., and the Republic of Ireland.
The border is a highly sensitive issue. Allowing the free flow of people is a major pillar of the peace process that largely endeddecades of violenceknown as “The Troubles.” The conflict involving Irish Republican and British Loyalist militants and U.K. security forces left almost 3,600 people dead before a 1998 peace accord.
Much of Tuesday’s violence took place in working-class areas where former paramilitary groups still hold considerable sway over the streets.
Last week a separate caseof a university studentwho was stabbed to death in Southampton, England, in December was seized on by activists andU.S. Vice President JD Vance, who blamed immigration for the violence, an idea rejected by Starmer and other British politicians.
Henry Nowak, who was white, was killed by Vickrum Digwa, a Sikh who falsely claimed to police that he was the victim of a racist assault by Nowak. When police officers arrived, they initially treated the wounded Nowak as a suspect before noticing his injury and trying to resuscitate him.
Digwa was convicted of murder and sentenced last week to life in prison with a minimum 21-year term. A protest over Nowak’s death turned violent, with some attacking police with chairs and rocks. Several people were charged with violent disorder.
Lawless reported from London. Brian Melley contributed to this story.

item.title: 涉嫌以色列间谍的神秘失踪使黎巴嫩国家陷入困境
item.description:
BEIRUT (AP) — As Israeli warplanespounded Beirut’s southern suburbslast March and residents fled in panic, one man found his opportunity. Amid the chaos, he slipped out of his imprisonment in a Hezbollah cell and made his way to the green hills overlooking the Lebanese capital.
There, in the posh diplomatic quarter of Baabda, he disappeared inside the gates of the Ukrainian Embassy.
Where he is now is a mystery, tangled up in an ongoing spy game as Hezbollah attempts to root outIsraeli intelligenceoperatives that have infiltrated the militant group.
The man identified by Lebanese officials as Khaled al-Aydi is said to be a Palestinian refugee from Syria who also holds Ukrainian citizenship. He had been detained by Hezbollah in the Beirut suburbs and accused by Lebanese officials of being part of a thwarted Israeli intelligence plot to carry out bombings and assassinations.
Details of al-Aydi’s escape and a Lebanese military court’s case against him were provided by three judicial officials and two senior security officials in Lebanon who spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to comment publicly. A senior political official in Hezbollah also provided details.
Al-Aydi’s disappearance could have political implications for the Lebanese government, which has largely remained silent about the case.
If evidence were to emerge that al-Aydi escaped Lebanon with help from the government, it could inflame tensions with Hezbollah’s largely Shiite Muslim base. The government alreadyfaces scrutinyfor directly negotiating with Israel, which has been engaged infierce fighting with Hezbollahsince the early days ofthe Iran war.
The Ukrainian Embassy asked Lebanese authorities in March to facilitate al-Aydi’s departure from the country after he escaped Hezbollah detention, according to a Lebanese government document obtained by The Associated Press. But Lebanon’s General Security agency refused, saying a judicial warrant for his arrest had been issued in September 2025, according to the document.
Israel’s Mossad intelligence agency declined to comment. Ukraine’s Foreign Ministry also declined to comment.
A Ukrainian official with knowledge of the case said al-Aydi is not in the Ukrainian Embassy or its compound in Lebanon. The official, who spoke on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the situation, would not say where al-Aydi is — and out of concern for the security of the embassy and its personnel, would not say whether al-Aydi was ever in the embassy, or whether Ukraine helped him escape.
Using human and high-tech surveillance, Israel has cultivated far-reaching intelligence networks in Lebanon. That has helped it carry out dramatic operations against Hezbollah.
In the most elaborate example, Israel infiltrated Hezbollah’s supply chain and sent the Iran-backed militant groupthousands of booby-trapped pagersand walkie-talkies. Israel remotely detonated the devices in September 2024, killing at least 37 people. Days later, Israeli airstrikes killed Hezbollah’s longtime leader, Hassan Nasrallah, while he was hiding in a heavily fortified bunker.
Even before that, Israel’s intelligence within Hezbollah allowed it to hit the group’s senior leaders and field commanders “with relative ease,” said Nicholas Blanford, an expert on the militant group at the Atlantic Council.
Since the 2024 Israel-Hezbollah war, Hezbollah and Lebanese authorities have cracked down on alleged spy networks. About 50 people have been convicted and are serving sentences, while others remain under investigation, the judicial officials said.
“We were successful in detecting many spy networks, and the state was also successful in this matter,” Hezbollah political officialWafiq Safa, said. But “the Israelis are always working to recruit young Lebanese people from all communities.”
Many alleged spy networks have involved current or former Hezbollah members or individuals with family ties to the group.
Al-Aydi, in contrast, was an outsider. He had Ukrainian citizenship through his mother, according to the Lebanese government document AP obtained. It is not known how he was allegedly recruited by Israel.
Hundreds of thousands of Syrians came to Lebanon for refuge during Syria’s 14-year civil war. But Al-Aydi entered the country in August 2025 on a flight from Ethiopia, one of the Lebanese security officials said.
While Hezbollah began in the 1980s as a small guerrilla operation fighting Israel’s occupation of southern Lebanon, it greatly expanded after its 2006 war with Israel, making it “easier for the Israelis to penetrate,” Blanford said. The group’s entry into the Syrian civil war further exposed it, as recruitment standards were lowered, he said.
Lebanon’seconomic crisisalso aided Israel’s recruitment efforts, Blanford said.
Cases filed in Lebanon’s military court describe operatives being paid between $2,500 and $20,000 to provide intelligence on Hezbollah weapons depots and political offices. Many of the alleged agents were recruited by Israeli handlers through social media, judicial officials said.
One high-profile case was Mohammad Hadi Saleh, a singer and prominent religious performer within circles connected to Hezbollah. He was arrested in May 2025 and charged with providing the Mossad with maps and coordinates of key Hezbollah sites later struck in Israeli operations. He is in jail awaiting trial.
“It’s ironic that they (Hezbollah) were spending a lot of time accusing their opponents of being Israeli spies, and it turns out that the spies were actually from within the organization and its support base,” said Mohanad Hage Ali, a senior fellow at the Malcolm H. Kerr Carnegie Middle East Center in Beirut.
Recruitment efforts continue. During the latest war, Israel has dropped leaflets over Lebanon with QR codes that, according to the Lebanese army, direct people to an Israeli military unit tasked with recruiting agents.
Lebanon’s General Security said in October it had broken up a network planning bombings and assassinations in Lebanon, including an operation meant to target events for the one-year commemoration of Nasrallah’s death. Authorities discovered a motorcycle rigged with explosives and a car modified to hold explosives, security and judicial officials said.
Al-Aydi and six others, all Lebanese, were charged. One of the six also escaped, and the others are in a Lebanese jail awaiting trial, the judicial officials said. Only al-Aydi was being held by Hezbollah, likely because he was seen as a high-value catch.
The military court alleges the operation was orchestrated by a Mossad handler living in Germany who communicated with others through encrypted applications. The court sent a summons to the Ukrainian Embassy that went unanswered.
Safa said there was an unsuccessful attempt to smuggle al-Aydi out of Lebanon to Syria. He did not elaborate.
The two senior Lebanese security officials said al-Aydi is believed to have left the country. It was not clear whether he crossed into Syria, where officials said they had no information about him.
Relations between the Lebanese government and Hezbollah are at a low point. The government was angered by the militant group’s unilateral decision to enter another war with Israel, while Hezbollah is furious the government has chosen to negotiate a ceasefire and potentially wider security and political agreement directly with Israel.
Al-Aydi’s escape could exacerbate tensions and put the Lebanese state in a difficult situation.
If Lebanese authorities refused to let al-Aydi leave the country, the U.S. and Ukraine were “well-positioned to exert significant pressure” to secure his release, Hage Ali said. On the other hand, if the state is seen to have let al-Aydi escape, it would face “public anger, predominantly among Lebanese Shia” sympathetic to Hezbollah, which could use that emotion to inflame internal tensions, he said.
Associated Press writers Samya Kullab in Kyiv, Ukraine, and Josef Federman in Jerusalem, contributed to this report.

item.title: 联合国核监督委员会要求伊朗紧急合作并进入核设施
item.description:
维也纳（美联社）——联合国原子能监督机构委员会周三要求伊朗与该机构充分合作，提供有关其近武器级核材料库存的完整信息，并允许其检查人员进入伊朗核设施。
国际原子能机构理事会通过的一项决议还表示，提供信息和访问权限是“必要且紧迫的”，以便能够核实不存在“核材料的转移”。
据不愿透露姓名的外交官透露，国际原子能机构理事会由 35 名成员组成，其中有 21 个国家在维也纳国际原子能机构总部投票支持了该决议。
俄罗斯、中国和尼日尔表示反对，10个国家投了弃权票，1个国家因拖欠票款而没有投票。
该决议由法国、英国、德国和美国提出。
一位因无权谈论敏感问题而要求匿名的西方高级外交官表示，该决议“旨在对伊朗持续施加外交压力，要求其遵守其法律保障义务。”
该决议出台之际，中东紧张局势加剧，美国周三早些时候对伊朗发动空袭，德黑兰则向该地区国家进行还击。不断升级的袭击有可能破坏结束战争的努力，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普警告称，伊朗政府将为陷入僵局的和平谈判“付出代价”。
自以色列和美国在 2025 年 6 月为期 12 天的战争中袭击了伊朗的核设施以来，伊朗一直没有允许国际原子能机构视察员进入受袭击影响的核设施——尽管根据《核不扩散条约》，德黑兰在法律上有义务与监督机构合作。
自六月爆炸事件以来，该机构还无法核实近武器级铀库存的状况。
据国际原子能机构称，伊朗拥有 440.9 公斤（972 磅）的浓缩铀库存，纯度高达 60%，距离 90% 的武器级水平仅几步之遥。
国际原子能机构总干事拉斐尔·马里亚诺·格罗西最近在接受美联社采访时警告说，如果伊朗决定将其计划武器化，那么伊朗的库存可能会让其制造多达 10 枚核弹。他表示，这并不意味着伊朗拥有这样的武器。
伊朗表示，它并不追求核武器，其计划完全用于和平目的。
伊朗驻国际原子能机构大使礼萨·纳杰菲在国际原子能机构董事会外对记者发表讲话，谴责了周三的决议，并表示该文件将伊朗的局势描述为“非常正常，就好像什么也没发生过一样”。
他表示，“在当前侵略者的袭击和持续威胁造成的前所未有的安全环境下，伊朗正常实施保障措施的法律、技术和操作基础已被破坏。”
纳杰菲还表示，伊朗已允许国际原子能机构进入“所有未受影响的设施”，并表示该决议“忽视了伊朗与该机构的所有合作，即使是在战争条件下也是如此。”
该决议还对伊朗“未能纠正”过去12个月不遵守防扩散义务的行为“深感遗憾”。去年6月，国际原子能机构董事会发现伊朗20年来首次正式不遵守其保障协议——就在美国和以色列对伊朗核设施发动攻击之前。
这个问题的核心是联合国核监督机构对检查人员在伊朗多个未申报地点检测到的铀痕迹进行的长期调查。
自2019年以来，伊朗未能向该机构提供有关这种核材料的来源和当前位置的“技术上可信的答案”。
西方官员怀疑，铀痕迹可以提供进一步的证据，证明伊朗在 2003 年之前一直拥有秘密核武器计划。
周三的决议没有将伊朗提交联合国安理会考虑对其不遵守规定的国家实施更多制裁，上一次采取这一行动是在 2006 年 2 月，当时伊朗被发现不遵守规定。
然而，周三的决议却敞开了大门，指出国际原子能机构理事会“将随时准备采取进一步行动”，包括解决国际原子能机构提交联合国安理会审议的正式违规行为报告的“时间和内容”。
美联社获得多个私人基金会的财政支持。美联社对所有内容全权负责。在 AP.org 上查找美联社与慈善机构合作的标准、支持者名单和受资助的覆盖范围。

item.title: 文字记录显示，大陪审团因不同意针对“Broadview Six”的案件而被解雇
item.description:
In the days following a marked escalation in the Trump administration’s “Operation Midway Blitz” mass deportation campaign in early October — including the shooting of a U.S. citizen by a federal immigration agent and the National Guard’s deployment to Chicago — a panel of grand jurors gathered once again behind closed doors in the Dirksen Federal Courthouse.
That particular group was nearing the end of its 18-month grand jury service, which began in June 2024, and had so far spent hundreds of hours hearing evidence brought by various federal prosecutors and signing off on indictments. Assistant U.S. Attorney Sheri Mecklenburg, a near two-decade veteran of the Department of Justice, had appeared in front of that grand jury enough times to develop a sort of bond with the group.
That bond, she told the grand jurors on Oct. 9, 2025, led her to ask her superiors if she could wait to present “a very interesting case” to them.
“I said I want to go in front of the Thursday grand jury because I know you and I trust you, and you know me and you trust me, and I would never ask you to charge somebody if I didn’t think there was probable cause,” Mecklenburg told the grand jury. “And you know you’ve asked me before, ‘Well, what about this person?’ And I said, ‘I don’t charge people unless I’m absolutely sure.’”
With that, Mecklenburg introduced the case against a group of Democratic activists, local elected officials and candidates that would come to be known as the “Broadview Six.”
But the afternoon did not go smoothly, as laid out in grand jury transcripts released Tuesday in an extremely rare move as part of an inquiry into how the now-imploded case came to be in the first place.
Mecklenburg and her partner, newly minted Assistant U.S. Attorney Matthew Skiba, played video footage for the grand jury from a protest two weeks prior outside of a federal immigration facility in Chicago’s near-west suburb of Broadview. During the demonstration on the morning of Sept. 26, 2025, a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement vehicle advanced toward the ICE facility, driving slowly through the crowd, which responded by closing in on the SUV and banging on its sides. The encounter left the vehicle with broken windshield wipers and the word “PIG” scratched into its side, the grand jury was told.
But during that first session, grand jurors declined to indict the six protesters at the end of their session, handing up a rare “no bill” indicating members of the grand jury did not believe prosecutors presented enough evidence to establish probable cause for a crime.
Nevertheless, Mecklenburg and Skiba appeared in front of the grand jury again the next Thursday, Oct. 16, 2025. She began the afternoon session telling grand jurors that she “did not do my job” the previous week and didn’t explain the law “well enough” to secure an indictment.
Not long into the presentation, however, one grand juror made clear their feelings on having to hear the same case again.
“I heard this case like last week and I thought it was a crock of s— then and I still think it is,” the grand juror said.
Mecklenburg then dismissed the grand juror, telling them to “have a good evening.”
Prior to that exchange, transcripts show another grand juror asked the prosecutors if they had “unlimited tries” to bring the case for indictment by the grand jury. Mecklenburg portrayed confidence that they were “going to be just fine,” and Skiba joked that, “I think the saying is the second time is the charm.”
But it would, in fact, take three tries before the grand jury ultimately indicted the group on Oct. 23.
U.S. District Judge April Perry OK’d the release of the grand jury transcripts nearly three weeks after the case fell apart in dramatic fashion in her courtroom just days before it was set to go to trial.
Lawyers for the protesters had been pushing to see the transcripts for months, but their calls got louder in March after prosecutors suddenly dropped all charges against two of the six defendants in the face of pressure to clarify the indictment.
Then in late April, on the same day Perry called a hearing to ask prosecutors for the unredacted transcripts, prosecutors made another surprise announcement: They’d decided to drop the felony conspiracy charge. While defense attorneys framed it as a win for their clients, they also suggested the feds’ move was a strategic way to avoid having to hand over unredacted grand jury transcripts.
In the days leading up to the four remaining defendants’rare federal misdemeanor trialin late May, defense attorneys made a final plea for Perry to review the grand jury transcripts. And after she did, the consequences came swiftly.
After a closed-door hearing in which the judge rebuked prosecutors for both apparent misconduct in front of the grand jury and for having previously obscured parts of the transcripts that would have revealed the misconduct earlier, she canceled the trial.
According to minutes of that May 21 sealed hearing, Perry characterized Mecklenburg’s opening statement urging trust of the case as “putting her personal credibility and trustworthiness on the line in support of the charges” — also known as improper prosecutorial vouching.
That same day, U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros, President Trump’s highest-ranking deputy in Chicago, took the extraordinary step of appearing in Perry’s courtroom, moving to drop the remaining charges and taking responsibility for the alleged prosecutorial misconduct. Soon after, Mecklenburg was fired from her new job while other cases she’d previously overseen have come into question.
And Boutros has locked into a public battle with those who have called for his resignation, including Democratic members of Illinois’ congressional delegation, and those who have launched serious criticisms of his leadership, including more than 100 former federal prosecutors from the Northern District of Illinois.
In an open letter published Monday, 111 signatories, including two of Boutros’ predecessors who’ve held the job of Chicago’s top federal prosecutor, bemoaned that in the 14 months since Boutros was confirmed as U.S. attorney, “actions taken by leadership … have tarnished the reputation” of the office.
The letter alleged that “once-forbidden political considerations are infecting prosecutorial decisions” and urged Boutros to develop “the courage to stand above and apart from political fealty.” The accusations come just a few days after defense attorneys filed a motion seeking any possible evidence of pressure from White House officials on the U.S. attorney’s office in Chicago to secure an indictment in the case.
In response, Boutros shot backin a social media statementboasting that in his time as U.S. attorney, his office has dramatically increased the number of indictments over previous years, despite the extraordinary turnover in prosecutors leaving the office.
“In every metric, we’ve vigorously brought the full weight of this Office and the federal government against dangerous criminals and serious fraudsters who previously got a pass,” Boutros wrote. “That’s because irrespective of factionalism, tribalism, or politics, the duty of a prosecutor is to charge good cases against worthy targets and to bring enough cases to discourage the public from committing crimes and to incapacitate specific offenders from re-offending.”
The U.S. attorney also claimed that when he began his job in April 2025, he “found an Office that was not well,” having “fallen to last place in virtually every metric tracked by the Department of Justice and the federal courts,” including indictments per prosecutor, charged defendants per prosecutor, criminal cases per federal judge and case processing time.
But Boutros did not address the ethical concerns voiced by the 111 former federal prosecutors who signed the letter, including their claims that the “special report” the U.S. attorney’s office published last week that revealed Boutros had spoken to the Broadview Six grand jury “raises more questions than answers.”
“He took the extraordinary step of questioning the grand jurors about whether they were ‘struggling’ with immigration cases after they had previously rejected charges in the Broadview Six case,” the letter read. “The message he delivered effectively signaled that the head of the U.S. Attorney’s Office is unhappy with us and we did something wrong. That message is inappropriate.”
The transcripts released Tuesday didn’t include Boutros’ approximately four-minute speech to the grand jury at the beginning of their Oct. 23, 2025, session, but Mecklenburg’s first recorded remarks echoed them.
“If you still feel like you are operating from feelings that prevent you from deliberating fairly with your fellow Grand Jurors and from applying the facts — the law to the facts here, then tell your fellow Grand Jurors that you can’t deliberate,” she admonished.
Mecklenburg then launched into a public acknowledgment of having talked to two grand jurors outside of the grand jury room prior to the beginning of the session, which she said “I’m not supposed to do.”
In both cases, she claimed the grand jurors apologized to her for their comments the previous week. It’s unclear whether either of the men Mecklenburg referenced were the same grand jurors she dismissed during the Oct. 16, 2025, session.
“The Grand Juror apologized to me from last week, and I told the Grand Juror that I accepted his apology,” Mecklenburg said of a grand juror she ran into in the elevator. “I wasn’t mad. I understand people have feelings. The Grand Juror said at that time that he did have feelings, and he’s sure that he’s right, but he shouldn’t have walked out the way he did.”
Transcripts from that previous week’s session detail both dismissals.
Before characterizing the case as “a crock of s—,” the same grand juror asked Mecklenburg if she was “actually presenting any new actual facts or just a different viewpoint on your side?”
The prosecutor responded that she was “feeling the skepticism already” and asked the grand juror whether they were “going to be able to listen with an open mind,” urging them to “tell me the truth.”
“Okay,” Mecklenburg said. “Then you have to go.”
Later, another grand juror told Mecklenburg, “I don’t think I can vote” on the indictment.
In contrast to her earlier dismissal of the other grand juror, Mecklenburg merely said, “Okay,” and then asked the foreperson if the grand jury would still have 16 members after the second departure, the minimum number of grand jurors needed for a quorum.
In his May 21 appearance in front of Perry, Boutros said he was aware of Mecklenburg’s dismissal of grand jurors at the time, telling the judge that “as soon as I became aware of it, I called off that grand jury session,” he said.
Indeed, the Oct. 16, 2025 session’s transcript ends abruptly in the middle of testimony from the ICE employee who drove the SUV into the crowd of protesters.
But Boutros claimed that he was unaware of Mecklenburg’s alleged prosecutorial vouching nor her communications with grand jurors outside the grand jury room until late April. When he found out, he said, he made the decision to drop the felony conspiracy charge.
From the beginning, grand jurors made clear their doubts about the case. During the first session on Oct. 9, 2025, members of the grand jury only heard from an FBI agent, and not the ICE employee who drove the SUV.
“Couldn’t he have stopped?” one asked, to which Mecklenburg reminded the grand jury of an FBI agent’s testimony to the group that the driver, an ICE employee, was “afraid to stop” for fear of the crowd breaking the vehicle’s windows and pulling him out.
“So if the person comes and stands in front of my car, do I have the right to drive against him?” a grand juror asked a little later, sparking a back-and-forth with Mecklenburg.
“We are not going — we don’t need to address that today because it didn’t happen,” she said. “It did not happen that he drove into any of them such that any of them were actually knocked over, hit.”
When the grand juror retorted that the reason no one was injured was “just luck,” Mecklenburg vehemently disagreed.
“No, it wasn’t luck. Oh, no, it wasn’t — sir, it was not luck,” she said. “It was his calmness and his judgment to drive as slowly as possible as he could and not hit anybody. It wasn’t just luck,” she said, according to the transcript.
Later this week, Perry is also likely to release the portion of the Oct. 9, 2025, grand jury transcript in which that FBI agent testified. The judge said she “was a little surprised” to see the agent’s testimony included in the parties joint filing Friday laying out the redactions they agreed to.
“The FBI agent is laying out the government’s case against you,” Perry said during Tuesday morning’s hearing. “If it’s released, it’s out there in the world forever.”
Without making explicit reference to it, the judge alluded to Boutros’ statements in open court late last month just moments after he said his office would be dropping the entire case — a few days before it was set for trial.
Though the U.S. attorney took responsibility for the apparent prosecutorial misconduct from his deputies on the case, Boutros also said the just-cleared defendants’ alleged actions were “unacceptable in a civilized society.” At the time, Perry told Boutros that he was “significantly undercutting your mea culpa here by standing behind the charges and continuing to vilify these particular defendants.”
The former prosecutors’ Monday letter characterized Boutros statement that day of violating “DOJ protocol and basic considerations of fairness” and criticizing his office-wide email that was quicklyobtained by the Chicago Tribune.
“By portraying the episode as a courageous effort by individual prosecutors, himself, and top supervisors, and by reaffirming his personal belief that a crime was committed, it sent an entirely wrong message to impressionable and inexperienced AUSAs,” the letter said.
On Tuesday, Perry warned that the agent’s testimony “will perhaps be used” against the former defendants “if the government continues to persist that crime was well-founded and charges were just.”
But defense attorneys said they were in favor of releasing the FBI agent’s testimony, while Terence Campbell, a lawyer for Andre Martin, one of the Broadview Six defendants, even advocated for the release of audio recordings from the grand jury proceedings.
“Let’s release the audio,” he said. “Let’s let everyone hear what was said.”
“Let’s start with the transcript,” Perry replied.
This story was originally published byCapitol News Illinoisand distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

item.title: 俄亥俄州托莱多音乐节发生大规模枪击事件后，警方将两名嫌疑人归零
item.description:
俄亥俄州托莱多（美联社）——俄亥俄州警方正在寻找一名 20 岁男子，该男子与在一个拥挤的社区节日期间两个敌对团体之间发生的枪击事件有关，该枪击事件造成 12 人受伤，同时当局请求帮助确认一名被他们称为“第二枪手”的人。
托莱多警察局周三表示，卡·奈·泰勒 (Ka Nye Taylor) 因 11 项重罪袭击罪被通缉，四天前，公园内布满活动帐篷、音乐舞台和餐车的枪击事件发生四天后。
警方周三公布了另一名身穿黑衣的男性嫌疑人参加音乐节的照片。泰勒或其家人的电话号码无法立即获得或在在线目录中找到。
托莱多警察局长迈克尔·特伦德尔(Michael Troendle)在周二的新闻发布会上表示，枪击事件是在音乐节上有人被擒抱和袭击后爆发的，导致一人开枪，另一人开枪回应。
当局称，被枪杀的 12 人中，有 3 人参与了争吵，其余都是旁观者。伤者年龄从十几岁到一名60多岁不等。截至周二，除三人外，其他人均已出院。
警察局长和其他城市官员赞扬了迅速帮助受害者的警察和好心人。
消防救援队长阿利森·阿姆斯特朗说：“我们看到陌生人对刚刚看到的暴力事件感到震惊和恐惧，他们立即采取行动。”“他们通过放置止血带、包扎伤口、施加压力和安慰受害者来帮助其他人，直到更多的帮助到达。”
阿姆斯特朗说，一半的受害者被警察或旁观者用止血带送到医院。
数百人参加了老西区音乐节，这是托莱多历史街区一年一度的为期两天的庆祝活动，覆盖多个街区，以现场音乐、摊贩和家庭旅游为特色。托莱多位于俄亥俄州西北部，靠近伊利湖西缘，距底特律西南约 55 英里（90 公里）。
周日，音乐节的其余活动被取消。组织者表示，继续整个周末是“富有同情心、负责任的或不可能的”。
拉默从新罕布什尔州康科德报道。

item.title: 南加州一名被卷入大海的少女的搜索范围扩大
item.description:
加利福尼亚州拉古纳海滩（美联社）——周三，由于高浪不断袭击海岸，一名十几岁女孩在南加州海滩散步时被大浪卷走，搜救工作扩大了。
拉古纳海滩海事安全负责人凯·邦德周三表示，周二晚上，这名女孩、她的母亲和兄弟正在拉古纳海滩市金银岛海滩的沙滩上行走，当时他们被卷入大海。
看到这家人被冲走的两个人救出了母子俩，目前情况稳定。他说，一名帮助这家人的男子随后被一名城市救生员从高达约 10 英尺（3 米）高的海浪中救出。
“我们正在扩大搜索范围，将邻近的海滩包括在内，我们正在使用潜水员、救援船和空中资源来搜索该地区。但目前我们还没有找到她，”邦德说。
邦德说，拉古纳海滩海上安全部门正在获得奥兰治县治安官港口巡逻队和美国海岸警卫队的帮助。
美国国家气象局在周三的冲浪报告中称，南加州的许多海滩预计至少在周四晚上将出现 5 至 8 英尺（1.5-2.5 米）的海浪，有些地方甚至更高。该部门表示，海滨城市马里布可能会看到高达 10 英尺（3 米）的海浪。
美国国家气象局气象学家菲利普·贡萨尔维斯 (Philip Gonsalves) 表示，在拉古纳海滩市所在的奥兰治县，海浪高度通常低于 6 英尺（约 2 米）。他表示，奥兰治县的海浪已经达到 10 英尺的峰值，预计在未来 24 小时内将开始下降。
他说，随着南半球冬季风暴的巨浪到达该地区，朝南或西南海滩的海浪特别高。

item.title: 南希·梅斯在南卡罗来纳州初选垫底后，不可预测的职业生涯悬而未决
item.description:
南卡罗来纳州哥伦比亚（美联社）——经过十年动荡的南卡罗来纳州和国家政治，众议员南希·梅斯在该州共和党州长初选中以第五名的遥遥领先的成绩，给这个国家一位毫不掩饰的政治家留下了一个不确定的未来。
她的竞选活动反映了她坎坷的职业生涯。梅斯在严厉批评唐纳德·特朗普总统 2021 年 1 月 6 日美国国会大厦袭击事件后，寻求了他的支持。她强调了她与其他共和党人为了公布杰弗里·爱泼斯坦调查文件而进行的斗争。
在周二初选前的最后几天，她呼吁制定一项法律，禁止任何非美国出生的人担任政治职务或担任法官。她表示，另一位州长候选人罗姆·雷迪没有资格，因为他是入籍公民，母亲来自印度，父亲来自意大利。
“我不是来自印度的贫民窟，”梅斯本月在格林维尔县露面时说道。“我在美国出生和成长。”
到竞选结束时，她只是偶尔公开露面。她努力筹集资金，也没有出现在电视上。梅斯主要通过社交媒体进行交流——自 2017 年首次当选南卡罗来纳州众议院议员以来，她一直利用社交媒体来发挥自己的优势。
梅斯在周二晚上的败选演讲中没有透露她的下一步计划，尽管她周三在社交媒体上发帖称，她将在当前众议院任期结束时“重返私营部门”。她在州长决选中支持艾伦·威尔逊，尽管就在去年，她还指责威尔逊保护儿童性虐待被告。
“当孩子们需要他采取行动时，威尔逊却视而不见，”她说。
威尔逊将在 6 月 23 日的决选中对阵副州长帕梅拉·埃维特。埃维特得到了特朗普的支持，这促使梅斯在社交媒体上猛烈抨击。
“帕梅拉·埃维特没有得到唐纳德·特朗普的认可，”梅斯错误地写道。“不要相信她的谎言。”梅斯发布了一张人工智能生成的她与特朗普合影的照片。
梅斯与特朗普的关系可以追溯到十年前，而且像许多共和党人一样，也有起有落。
她在 2016 年特朗普竞选活动中担任现场主任，特朗普支持她在 2020 年首次竞选国会议员，尽管是在她赢得共和党提名之后。
但梅斯一抵达华盛顿，他们的关系就破裂了。这位新任众议院议员试图让特朗普对 1 月 6 日事件负责，并表示他的“全部遗产”已被“消灭”，之后他给梅斯贴上了“疯狂”的标签，并征求初选挑战者与她较量。特朗普支持其中一位并为其竞选，但梅斯——未来的特朗普联合竞选经理克里斯·拉奇维塔(Chris LaCivita)担任她的媒体顾问——最终获胜，特朗普继续支持她的大选竞选。
当特朗普寻求 2024 年重返白宫时，梅斯直到新罕布什尔州初选前一天才支持他而不是前南卡罗来纳州州长妮基·黑利。
梅斯在共和党全国代表大会的黄金时段演讲中称赞特朗普，并在赢得连任后，在州长竞选初期将自己称为“穿高跟鞋的特朗普”。
但另一次断裂即将出现。今年春天，在寻求特朗普的支持时，梅斯与国会民主党人一起迫使司法部公布与杰弗里·爱泼斯坦有关的文件。
特朗普接着支持埃维特，称她对他的支持“从未动摇”。埃维特在 2024 年竞选初期就支持特朗普。
四名国会共和党人是推动释放请愿书以迫使文件被公开的最初小组的成员。梅斯和众议员托马斯·马西输掉了竞选，众议员马乔里·泰勒·格林在一月份辞职。
梅斯在周二落败后发表的一份长篇声明中表示，她“与两党的富人和有权势的人较量”，并“投票决定公布爱泼斯坦的文件，但因此失去了一些支持”。
梅斯从高中退学，在获得文凭之前在华夫饼屋担任服务员。后来她就读于城堡并成为第一位从该州军事学院毕业的女性。近年来，她谈到了保护性侵犯受害者的重要性，并分享了青少年时期被强奸的故事。
在南卡罗来纳州众议院开始政治生涯后，梅斯在 2020 年赢得了查尔斯顿周边美国众议院席位（该席位已转给民主党一个任期），受到了共和党人的广泛赞誉。
“对于今天在场的那些昨天可能不在场的人，我请求一个机会——一个向你们证明我将成为一位富有同情心的领导者、一位好的倾听者、一位独立的思想家的机会，”梅斯当时说道。
金纳德从华盛顿报道。比尔·巴罗 (Bill Barrow) 来自亚特兰大。

item.title: 特朗普对生活成本上升有一个令人惊讶的新看法：“我喜欢通货膨胀”
item.description:
华盛顿（美联社）——周三，唐纳德·特朗普总统展示了他如何学会不再担心通货膨胀，用他自己的话来说，就是“热爱”通货膨胀。
当被问及关于 5 月份消费者价格指数比去年上涨 4.2% 的新报告时，总统对这一具有挑战性的消息采取了令人惊讶的乐观态度。特朗普并没有像之前那样将负担能力问题视为民主党发起的“骗局”。他也没有声称他正在降低生活成本。
相反，在政府表示通胀飙升至 2023 年 4 月以来的最高水平后，特朗普对这些数字表示赞扬。
“你知道我真正喜欢什么吗？”特朗普说道。“我喜欢通货膨胀。”
鉴于 11 月中期选举之前的选民已将经济列为最关心的问题，并在该问题上给特朗普打了低分，因此这是一个出乎意料的决定。他在镜头前发表评论后几分钟内，民主党人迅速在社交媒体上进行宣传。
特朗普在 2024 年竞选中承诺迅速消除通货膨胀，但他现在的论点是，价格上涨仅仅是伊朗战争提高能源成本的结果。周三，他声称，由于一项秘密军事行动，救援工作已经在进行中。据他所说，霍尔木兹海峡运送了 1 亿桶石油。霍尔木兹海峡是全球 20% 石油供应的主要运输通道，但自 2 月下旬以来，该海峡实际上已因战争而关闭。
“特朗普真的说过，‘我喜欢通货膨胀。’在镜头前。让全美国都听到，”参议院民主党领袖查克·舒默很快在 X 上发帖称。“他对你的蔑视是无止境的。”
众议院民主党领袖哈基姆·杰弗里斯 (Hakeem Jeffries) 在 X 上表示，鉴于特朗普对通货膨胀的热爱，“我们终于找到了唐纳德·特朗普像爱自己一样喜欢的东西。”
俄亥俄州民主党众议员艾米莉亚·赛克斯在听证会上迅速向能源部长克里斯·赖特施压，询问他是否也喜欢通货膨胀。
“我喜欢终结伊朗拥有核武器的能力，”赖特回答道。他在迫于压力后才承认：“不，我更希望通胀更低。”
当被问及特朗普的具体评论时，赖特说：“他是一个有趣、夸张的人，发挥了巨大的领导作用。”
特朗普声称，秘密发货是油价在 4 月初突破每桶 110 美元后跌破每桶 90 美元的原因。
特朗普说：“我今天才第一次宣布，但我们已经消耗了数百万桶石油，每晚数百万桶。”
总统在社交媒体上表示，该任务于上个月开始，“已导致超过 1 亿桶石油通过海峡并进入公开市场。超过 200 艘商船已安全通过该海峡。”没有立即可用的数据来支持这一总数，也不清楚美国军方发挥了什么作用。
考虑到这一数字，战前每天平均有 2000 万桶石油通过该海峡，这意味着特朗普的任务相当于正常五天的石油运输量。
在回应新的通胀报告时，白宫指出，5月份的一些支出较上个月有所下降：例如新车、处方药和汽车保险的价格。但当总体通胀数据与时薪变化相结合时，出现了一个暗淡的迹象，表明人们的消费能力相对于收入已经下降。
白宫发言人库什·德赛在一封电子邮件中表示：“特朗普总统一直坚称，一旦伊朗问题得到解决，石油和天然气价格以及整体通胀将会大幅下降，政府将继续推动我们的负担能力议程，使美国人能够保留更多辛苦赚来的钱。”
但金融市场对特朗普声称他通过让油轮通过海峡来降低油价的说法持谨慎态度——这一说法是在美国也对伊朗发动空袭以及德黑兰向该地区国家进行还击之际发出的。
美国原油期货周三上涨约 4%，收于每桶近 92 美元。

item.title: 索马里足球裁判被美国拒绝入境参加世界杯，被视为英雄而受到欢迎
item.description:
索马里摩加迪沙（美联社）——周三，一名被美国拒绝入境参加世界杯比赛的索马里著名足球裁判在回国时受到了一群支持者和官员的热烈欢迎。
被评为 2025 年非洲最佳男裁判的奥马尔·阿尔坦 (Omar Artan) 表示，他计划参加下一届世界杯，并敦促索马里年轻人为自己的国家感到自豪。
在列入国际足联的世界杯最终名单后，阿坦将成为索马里第一位担任世界杯裁判的裁判。
周六，由于未具体说明的“审查问题”，他在迈阿密国际机场被拒绝入境美国。海关和边境保护局在一份声明中表示，但没有详细说明问题。国际足联随后将他从赛事裁判名单中删除。
据负责办理该签证的索马里驻肯尼亚大使馆称，阿坦上周获得了前往美国的签证。美国与墨西哥和加拿大共同主办世界杯，阿坦将在迈阿密的训练基地与其他世界杯裁判会面。
抵达首都摩加迪沙后，他感谢索马里政府和民众以及国际足联的支持。
“我向你们保证，上帝保佑，我会参加下一场，”他说，机场数百名支持者挥舞着索马里国旗。“我希望索马里公众对此感到安慰并保持信心。”
周三晚些时候，数千名球迷挤满了摩加迪沙体育场，为阿尔坦举行欢迎仪式，爱国歌曲在体育场内回响，支持者挥舞着国旗为他加油。
总理哈姆扎·阿卜迪·巴雷 (Hamza Abdi Barre) 还接待了阿尔坦，他在 X 上的一篇帖子中写道，这位裁判“已经赢得了数百万人的心，并确保了他在历史上的地位”。
“他致力于确保足球是由功绩决定的，但命运却剥夺了他应得的舞台，”巴雷说。
美国拒绝国际足联指定的比赛官员进入世界杯主办国的许可这一极不寻常的举动引起了全世界的愤怒，并引发了一些球迷对美国主办世界杯的能力的质疑。
索马里是特朗普政府打击移民行动中受到新旅行限制的近 40 个国家之一。
周三，联合国最高人权官员呼吁对移民政策进行“大规模反思”，尤其是世界杯期间美国的移民政策。
在阿坦抵达亚丁阿德国际机场前几个小时，数百名支持者、政府官员和索马里足球界的成员聚集在一起。
当他下船时，挥舞着索马里国旗的支持者聚集在他周围，然后将国旗盖在他身上。
随后，他在警察的护送下前往机场的贵宾航站楼，在那里受到索马里体育部长和其他政要的欢迎，并向记者发表讲话。
“我们所有人都有责任捍卫索马里的名字，”阿坦说。“索马里属于我们，无论它的状态是好是坏。那面旗帜属于我们，那本护照属于我们。”
在这个国家，数十年的战争和与基地组织有关联的青年党极端主义组织的崛起限制了索马里许多人的潜力，阿坦的否认带来了失望，但提醒人们，如果他们追逐自己的梦想，一切都是可能的。
来自邻国埃塞俄比亚的世界卫生组织总干事谭德塞周二写道，阿坦在今年世界杯上的预期里程碑“无论如何都不会改变”。“你达到了职业生涯的顶峰，并通过到达那里激励了一代人，而被排除在你应得的球场之外并不会改变这一点。”

item.title: 尼克斯队在落后29分的情况下完成创纪录反弹，以107-106击败马刺队，以3-1领先总决赛
item.description:
NEW YORK (AP) — A record-breaking comeback, capped off by what could go down as a legendary play.
The long road back to the top of the NBA is almost complete for the New York Knicks, and the step they took Wednesday night was unforgettable.
The Knicks came from 29 points down and moved to the brink of their first championship since 1973 by beating the San Antonio Spurs 107-106 in Game 4 of the finals onOG Anunoby’s tip-inwith 1.2 seconds remaining.
“That has to be the most iconic shot in the history of New York basketball,” Knicks coach Mike Brown said.
It’s certainly high on the list — as high as Anunoby leaped when Jalen Brunson’s long 3-point shot bounced off the front of the rim, with his right hand stretching high to softly flick it in.
“Right hand from God,” Knicks center Karl-Anthony Towns said.
The Knicks, who have just two titles in their 80-year history and hadn’t even been to the NBA Finals since 1999, have a 3-1 lead and three chances to win the best-of-seven series — starting with Game 5 on Saturday night in San Antonio.
It looked impossible early, when the Spurs rolled to a 27-point halftime lead. But Brunson helped bring the Knicks back with 36 points and Anunoby finished with 33.
No team had come from more than 24 points down in a finals game, when Boston did it against the Lakers in 2008, since the NBA began keeping detailed play-by-play for all four quarters in 1997. The Spurs led 81-52 in the third quarter.
“We’re a resilient group. We’ve been through a lot,” Anunoby said. “We’ve come back plenty of times when we’re behind. Just staying with it, weathering the storm, not being too down or angry or frustrated.”
The only bigger comeback on record in any playoff game was 31 points by the Los Angeles Clippers against Golden State in Game 2 of a first-round series in 2019.
“You look at it when you’re down 29 of, ‘OK, let’s get it to 20.’ There’s three minutes left in the third quarter, we’re down 18, you’re thinking, ‘Let’s get it to 10,” forward Josh Hart said.
“In the fourth quarter, you’re like, this is winning time. Anything can happen.”
The Knicks had their 13-game winning streak snapped in Game 3 and seemed headed for a second straight defeat throughout the first half, when Victor Wembanyama and the Spurs opened the biggest halftime lead by a visiting team in the finals.
But the young Spurs, who made 11 of their first 16 3-pointers, went cold in the second half, going 3 for 17 behind the arc as the Knicks outscored them 58-30.
“We got on our heels — we missed some shots,” Spurs coach Mitch Johnson said. “It’s disappointing, to say the least.”
Delirious fans inside Madison Square Garden sang along to Journey’s “Don’t Stop Believin’” a few minutes after watching something that seemed almost impossible.
Wembanyamahad 24 points and 13 rebounds but shot just 9 for 25 from the field.
Road teams had won the first three games, only the second time that had happened in the finals. San Antonio was well on its way to making it 4 for 4.
President Donald Trump wasn’t at this game —Taylor Swift was— but thesame restrictions remained around Madison Square Gardenas when he attended Game 3. That angered the Knicks, who decided not to go forward with plans to hold an outdoor watch party outside the arena.
Inside the building in the first half, there wasn’t much for the hosts to be happy about, either.
But the Knicks gave themselves a chance by limiting the Spurs to 14 points on 4-for-20 shooting in the third quarter, using a 13-0 run to get back in it and cutting it to 90-75 heading to the fourth.
These Knicks, who erased a 22-point deficit in the fourth quarter against Cleveland in Game 1 of the Eastern Conference finals, just don’t quit. Even when the comeback seemed for naught when Stephon Castle was fouled after the Knicks had taken the lead and made two free throws to put San Antonio back ahead with 30 seconds left, the Knicks had one more rally in them.
Dylan Harper scored 21 points and De’Aaron Fox and Devin Vassell each had 18 for the Spurs, who will try to regroup and send the series back to New York for Game 6 next Tuesday. Only one team — Cleveland in 2016 — has recovered from a 3-1 deficit in the finals.
“I think it began before (the fourth quarter),” Wembanyama said of the Spurs’ collapse. “I can’t really explain it right now. I don’t know. ... We clearly weren’t the most hungry in the second half.”
Fans booed Wembanyama when he came on to the floor to warm up about an hour before the game and the Knicks tried to get rough with him, with Mitchell Robinson called for a flagrant foul for hitting him above the shoulders and Jose Alvarado reviewed for one after going below the belt.
Wembanyama — who was also called for a flagrant — stood up OK against the Knicks but will regret the two free throws he missed with 1:47 left and San Antonio leading 104-103.
The Spurs broke to a 12-2 lead, giving them a double-digit advantage in the first quarter of all four games. They kept pouring it on and led 41-22 after one, then extended it to 57-32 when Julian Champagnie’s 3-pointer made them 11 for 16 behind the arc.

item.title: 国际足联主席因凡蒂诺捍卫世界杯门票价格，表示球迷“应该对裁判拒绝美国入场感到冷静”
item.description:
MEXICO CITY (AP) — FIFA President Gianni Infantino defendedWorld Cupticket prices on Wednesday, saying “if we do something wrong, then probably everyone selling tickets in North America is doing something wrong.”
Infantino spoke with reporters during a rare question-and-answer session on the eve of the opening match of an expanded 48-nation, 104-game World Cup tournament. He defended record ticket prices, said FIFA was powerless to get the U.S. government to admit a Somali referee, and praised his ability to get Iran’s national soccer team into the United States.
FIFA priced tickets starting at $140 for group-stage games, but regular seats for the July 19 final outside New York were listed at up to $8,680 and hospitality seats at up to $73,200. It raised prices for thefinal to $10,990andthen $32,970.
After much criticism,FIFA offered $60 tickets to national federations for their regular supporters.Infantino said 130,000 tickets were offered in that category.
Four years ago at the tournament in Qatar, prices ranged from $69 to $1,607.
“If you sell it at a lower price point,” he said, “in this particular market it would have gone — which is perfectly legal in this country ... in secondary markets at much, much, much higher prices and where would the money go then? Well, to those who organize secondary markets or black market activities and not to football.”
Infantino said the average ticket price was below $500 for the tournament and was comparable to other U.S. sports during their playoffs, a claim that while true for resale prices does not appear to be accurate for list prices. Major League Baseball’s average World Series ticket price has been $350 to $400 in recent years and NFL prices last season averaged $230 for the wild-card round, $320 for divisional games, $450 for conference championships and $3,300 for the Super Bowl.
Infantino said he was unconcerned aboutticket probes by attorneys generalin California, New Jersey, New York and Texas.
“When it comes to these legal investigations or complaints that were made in some states in the U.S.,” he said, “we are very relaxed about it because before starting to sell 6 and a half or 7 million tickets we check what we do with the best lawyers, with the best experts. If we do something wrong, then probably everyone selling tickets in North America is doing something wrong, as well.”
Infantino pointed to the NBA Finals between the New York Knicks and San Antonio Spurs as an example of how other major American sporting events are experiencing a similar surge in prices.
The get-in price on FIFA’s resale site for the World Cup final is $9,805. The NBA Finals have had wildly varied get-in prices, ranging from a minimum of about $500 for the first two games in San Antonio to about $10,000 for Game 3 in New York. Game 4 in New York was much less, dropping to about $4,000 on Wednesday.
The Stanley Cup Final this year between teams in Las Vegas and Raleigh, North Carolina, has included a get-in price of at least $600 for each of the first four games of the best-of-seven series.
“We welcome every investigation,” Infantino said. “We are happy to present everything, and we are happy to make our case.”
Infantino said it was unfortunateSomali referee Omar Artanwas denied entry to the U.S.
“We don’t control everything. We try. We’ll discuss, we will speak, we’ll see. Maybe sometimes it’s good as well to just chill, relax,” he said. “We try to solve everything. Sometimes to immediately start screaming and shouting has the opposite effect of finding a solution. Believe me when I tell you, or don’t believe me if you don’t want, but we try always to find solutions, always. But then we need to respect that we are not the kings of the world who can rule over governments and police forces.”
Set to become thefirst Somali refereeat a World Cup, Artan was denied entry to the U.S. at Miami International Airport on Saturday over unspecified “vetting concerns,”U.S. Customs and Border Protectionsaid in a statement, without giving details of the concerns.
“Our world is a very aggressive world and security goes above everything and you need to respect the decisions which are taken,” Infantino said. “We are working behind the scenes.”
Infantino praised FIFA for working through details that allow Iran to play in the tournament at a time the U.S. is at war with that country. The Iranian team moved its training camp from the U.S. to Mexico and will fly to the United States just before matches.
“I think it has already been successful to bring Iran to play in America,” he said. “I don’t know who would have managed to do that. And of course everyone here believes it’s the right thing to do. But, again, we don’t live on the moon. We live on planet Earth and you have to deal with different situations.”
Infantino claimed the tournament will be “the biggest event probably in the history of mankind.”
“Without his engagement and his involvement I think it would have been impossible — as simple as that, it would’ve been impossible to organize a World Cup in the United States,” Infantino said. “He understood immediately the magnitude of the World Cup, the impact of the World Cup.”
FIFA is projecting $11 billion in revenue for the tournament,and Infantino said the governing body could have generated far more.
“We could put everything on pay. We would generate $30 billion revenues, probably,” he said. “But then you have billions of people in the world who would not be able to watch the World Cup.”
Four years ago,Infantino scolded criticsduring a news conference ahead of the opening match in Qatar. He lectured Europeans for criticizing Qatar’s human rights record during a bizarre appearance in which he claimed to feel gay, like a woman and a migrant worker, among other odd claims.
Now 56, Infantino has been FIFA president since 2016 andintends to seek another term next yearthat runs through 2031.
Infantino kept an empty seat at the news conference for Christophe Gleizes, aFrench freelance reporter given a seven-year prison sentencein Algeria last year over an interview with a soccer official accused of ties to a banned separatist movement.
AP Sports Writer Ronald Blum in New York contributed to this report.
AP World Cup coverage:https://apnews.com/hub/fifa-world-cup

item.title: 格伦·克洛斯最终将获得奥斯卡荣誉奖。雷德利·斯科特和动画师弗洛伊德·诺曼也会如此
item.description:
格伦·克洛斯最终将获得奥斯卡奖。
美国电影艺术与科学学院周三宣布，这位长期被认为是从未获奖的最佳演员之一，将与导演雷德利·斯科特和动画师弗洛伊德·诺曼一起在年度州长奖上获得荣誉奥斯卡奖。
该学院在一份声明中表示：“通过格伦·克洛斯非凡的作品，她无与伦比的情感范围使电影中一些最复杂的角色栩栩如生。”“弗洛伊德·诺曼是一位传奇动画师，他在其非凡的职业生涯中打破了障碍，激励了一代又一代的艺术家。雷德利·斯科特爵士是一位真正的梦想家，他数十年的遗产对全球电影和文化产生了不可估量的影响。”
79 岁的克洛斯的提名可以追溯到 1983 年，当时她凭借《盖普眼中的世界》首次获得提名。她还因在 1987 年的《致命诱惑》中扮演杀兔跟踪者一角而获得提名，最近又因 2020 年的《乡下挽歌》获得小金人奖。
她获得八项提名，与彼得·奥图尔并列获得提名最多的未获奖演员。
她几乎赢得了所有其他可以获得的主要奖项，包括三项艾美奖、三项托尼奖、三项格莱美奖和三项金球奖。
州长奖通常颁发给事业非凡的艺术家，但奥斯卡奖却没有竞争力。去年的获奖者汤姆·克鲁斯就是一个很好的例子。
斯科特现年 88 岁，曾执导过《异形》、《银翼杀手》和《角斗士》，几十年来史诗般的作品与其他人一样，将大众的成功和声望融为一体，尽管他获得了四项提名，其中包括凭借《塞尔玛与路易丝》和《黑鹰坠落》获得最佳导演提名，但他从未获奖。
诺曼 65 年的职业生涯始于 1956 年，当时他成为华特迪士尼动画工作室的第一位黑人动画师，为《睡美人》、《欢乐满人间》、《奇幻森林》和《罗宾汉》做出了贡献。几十年后，他拍摄了《花木兰》、《玩具总动员2》和《怪兽电力公司》。
学院表示，制片人克里斯汀·瓦琼 (Christine Vachon) 和帕梅拉·科夫勒 (Pamela Koffler) 将获得学院的欧文·G·撒尔伯格纪念奖，该奖颁发给“一位创意制片人，其作品体现了电影制作一贯的高品质”。
Vachon 和 Koffler 于 1995 年共同创立了位于纽约的独立制作中心 Killer Films。他们作为制片人的作品包括《海德薇与愤怒的英寸》、《一小时照片》和《五月十二月》。两部电影均凭借《前世》获得 2024 年奥斯卡最佳影片提名。Killer Films 的作品包括《天鹅绒金矿》、《幸福》、《男孩别哭》、《远离天堂》和《卡罗尔》。
学院声明称，两人“在美国独立电影中发挥着核心作用，倡导大胆、雄心勃勃和独特的故事讲述。”
所有获奖者将于 11 月 15 日在 Ovation Hollywood 的 Ray Dolby 宴会厅举行的颁奖典礼上接受颁奖，该宴会厅也是奥斯卡主要颁奖典礼的举办地。除了与获奖者一起工作的名人之外，该活动也越来越多地挤满了年轻明星，因为它已成为好莱坞颁奖季竞选活动的非官方拉开序幕。
州长奖以学院理事会而非国家领导人的名字命名，旨在表彰“终身成就的非凡成就、对任何学科的电影艺术和科学状况的杰出贡献，或对学院的杰出服务”。

item.title: 基思·厄本 (Keith Urban) 并没有打算录制游艇摇滚唱片。但随后他进入了“心流状态”
item.description:
纽约（美联社）——基思·厄本（Keith Urban）是一位乡村音乐明星，但敏锐的听众都知道他的歌曲创作风格在不同流派中都很流畅。尽管如此，肯定没有人预料到他 2026 年的专辑《Flow State》会发生转变。它包含一首由音乐传奇人物迈克尔·麦克唐纳 (Michael McDonald) 创作的原创歌曲，以及 10 首游艇摇滚翻唱歌曲。
该术语是对 70 年代中后期和 80 年代初发行的软摇滚的总称。想想普莱尔的《宝贝回来》、小格罗弗·华盛顿和比尔·威瑟斯的《只有我们两个人》等经典作品。
就连厄本也对他的转向感到惊讶。很少有人预见到它的到来。“我也没有，不，”他笑道。
事情是从录音开始的。Urban 购买并修复了纳什维尔的前 Tracking Room 工作室，并将其更名为 The Sound。为了打破这个空间，他聘请了一些会议音乐家。“我建议我们制作一首游艇摇滚歌曲，只是因为我一直喜欢这种松散定义的流派，而且这些歌曲很棒，编曲也很无懈可击，”他说。
Urban、制作人 Dann Huff 和乐队在一天内录制了两首歌。他们很着急，所以又预定了几场。然后还有一些。“我想，好吧，也许这会是一张 EP，介于专辑之间的东西，”他说。它不断增长，最终成为一个完整的版本。“它确实有了自己的生命。”
厄本说，他和他的团队纯粹是为了乐趣而演奏和录音。他说游艇摇滚是“这种精神的完美流派”——一种以一种轻松的存在感来定义的流派。
但对于他的第一张带封面的专辑，厄本并没有对破坏经典或试图完美复制它们感兴趣。“Flow State”存在于中间的某个地方，他的声音特征完好无损。扩展的尾声是实验的自然场所。以“夏日微风”为例。录制完这首曲目后，Urban 和工程师 Mark Dobson 决定用键盘和原声吉他来延长结尾。“我们事后组装了它，它就成了另一件作品，”他说。“那些时刻不断出现，我们可以在尊重原创的同时将其变成我们自己的。”
它在整张专辑中都很流行，包括唱片中的合作：Little Big Townon Walter Egan 的“Magnet and Steel”和John Mayeron Bread 的“The Guitar Man”。
《Flow State》唯一的原创曲目是《We Go Back》，这是一首怀旧的曲调，讲述了一对在生活中分离又重逢的情侣的故事。厄本在 2020 年与 BRELAND、萨姆·萨姆瑟和肖恩·斯莫尔一起创作了这首歌。“我对这些人说，‘天哪，如果我们要写一首游艇摇滚歌曲，想象一下迈克·麦克唐纳在副歌部分唱歌，’他回忆道。
快进到 2026 年，游艇摇滚翻唱专辑问世，Urban 的经理建议他与 Kenny Loggins 或 McDonald 合作。他们向麦当劳发送了“我们回去”，剩下的就是历史了。
“幸运的是，他喜欢它，”他说。“但是，六年后，当我们只是想象他多年前唱这首歌时，他真的在唱这首歌，真是太疯狂了。太超现实了。”
特别是考虑到厄本一开始就没有考虑为这张专辑创作原创作品——这些片段就自然而然地就位了。
Urban 描述他选择在“Flow State”中翻唱的歌曲现在唱起来感觉和最初发行时一样好。
“音乐几乎是缓解时代压力的解药。我认为它现在流行的原因与当时的原因完全相同，那就是——存在太多分歧，”他解释道。这些歌曲让 2026 年的观众有机会逃离现实并陶醉在音乐的欢乐中。
它们对于听众和厄本来说都是一剂良药。
今年一月，演员妮可·基德曼和厄本在结婚19年后正式离婚。文件称，这对夫妇经历了“婚姻困难和不可调和的分歧”。
厄本将《Flow State》描述为“一组意想不到的歌曲，也是一张非常意想不到的唱片，当它找到我时就找到了我，因为它很大程度上是当时正在发生的事情的 180 度翻转。”
“这张唱片被称为‘Flow State’是有充分理由的，”他继续说道。“这确实是关于不断的运动。是的，同时制作这是一张具有挑战性的唱片，因为听起来如此轻松。这在当时是相当的并置。但我真的很感激这张唱片的结果，显然我非常非常保护我的家人，而且我一直都是这样。”
至于“Flow State”，他说游艇摇滚歌曲可以将人们彼此联系起来，这一想法确实很美妙。“一个主题、一种感觉、一种情绪，让我们所有人呼气一分钟，抬头看到蓝天，只需三分三十秒，”他说。

item.title: 克里斯蒂亚·弗里兰（Chrystia Freeland）的“不可靠男友”为美加紧张局势提供了前排座位
item.description:
纽约（美联社）——一位经常与唐纳德·特朗普总统发生冲突的加拿大前政府高级官员正在写一本关于加拿大与美国紧张关系的书。
西蒙与舒斯特周三宣布，前副总理克里斯蒂亚·弗里兰的《不可靠的男朋友》将于 10 月 13 日上映。
“这是一本关于权力、民主以及当旧规则似乎不再适用时国家所做出的选择的书，”弗里兰在出版商发布的一份声明中说。“作为一名加拿大人，在政治动荡的岁月里与美国进行谈判，我站在历史性变革的前排。我想讲述我所看到的故事，以及它对未来的意义。”
弗里兰是俄罗斯和乌克兰问题专家，拥有哈佛大学和牛津大学学位，在从政之前已经是一名作家和记者。2015 年选举后，她被时任总理贾斯汀·特鲁多任命为国际贸易部长，并在接下来的十年中担任过多种职务。
2017年，在特朗普的第一个任期内，当各国致力于达成美国-墨西哥-加拿大协议时，她成为了总统的攻击目标。“我们对加拿大的谈判和谈判风格非常不满意。我们不太喜欢他们的代表，”特朗普当时表示。
2024-25 年，特朗普威胁对加拿大进口产品征收关税，并暗示加拿大作为美国第 51 个州的处境会更好，她称特朗普对该国的未来构成“生存威胁”。特朗普称她“完全有毒”和“可怕的人”。
弗里兰与特鲁多决裂，并于 2024 年因在如何回应特朗普方面存在分歧而辞去特鲁多内阁职务，并帮助迫使特鲁多辞职。此后，她在马克·卡尼总理的内阁任职，并担任乌克兰特使。七月，她将成为罗德之家的典狱长和牛津罗德信托基金会的首席执行官，负责监督这一备受尊敬的奖学金计划。她本人是1993年的罗德学者。

item.title: 本田因后悬架组件问题召回超过 88 万辆汽车
item.description:
本田正在召回超过 800,000 辆汽车，因为后悬架组件可能发生故障并导致驾驶员失去控制，从而增加撞车或受伤的可能性。
美国本田汽车公司表示，此次召回涉及部分 2016-2022 年款 Honda Pilot、2017-2023 年款 Ridgeline、2019-2023 年款 Passport 和 2014-2020 年款 Acura MDX 车辆。此次召回包括康涅狄格州、特拉华州、哥伦比亚特区、伊利诺伊州、印第安纳州、爱荷华州、肯塔基州、缅因州、马里兰州、马萨诸塞州、密歇根州、明尼苏达州、密苏里州、新罕布什尔州、新泽西州、纽约州、俄亥俄州、宾夕法尼亚州、罗德岛州、佛蒙特州、弗吉尼亚州、西弗吉尼亚州和威斯康星州出售的 880,514 辆汽车。
问题集中在后副车架上，它会在悬架安装点腐蚀并导致后悬架失效。本田估计列出的车辆中只有 1% 存在缺陷。
本田没有收到任何保修索赔，也没有与该问题相关的受伤或死亡报告。
作为补救措施，本田和讴歌经销商将检查后副车架，并在必要时安装加固套件，或者免费修理或更换后副车架部件，车主无需支付任何费用。
业主通知信预计将于 7 月 7 日寄出。
美国国家公路交通安全管理局的召回活动编号为 26V367000。本田此次召回的编号为 AOU 和 AOT。从 6 月 10 日开始，可在 NHTSA.gov 上搜索适用于本次召回的车辆识别号。
车主可以拨打 1-888-234-2138 联系本田客户服务。
